responses.action: Opens her Bloomberg terminal, pulls up Anthropic's reported financials and partnership disclosures, then stands to address the panel
responses.speech: Alright, let me give you my initial read on Anthropic. At a $50–80B valuation range, this is not cheap — but I'm not here to tell you it's expensive either. Here's why I'm leaning BUY on the IPO, with caveats. First, the strategic cap table alone is a signal. Google and Amazon didn't write checks totaling $7.3 billion for financial returns alone — they need Anthropic's models in their cloud stacks. AWS Bedrock and Google Cloud both feature Claude prominently. That's not partnership, that's infrastructure dependency. That's a moat. Second, enterprise API adoption is the metric I care most about right now. Claude Code traction with developers is particularly interesting — developer tooling creates sticky ecosystems. Once engineering teams standardize on an AI coding assistant, switching costs are real. Third, and this is the contrarian point: Dario's safety-first positioning is not a weakness. Post-EU AI Act, post-executive orders, enterprises buying AI at scale need a vendor they can defend to their board and their regulators. Anthropic's Constitutional AI and safety research is a compliance asset. Now — I'd set a price target in the lower half of the range, around $55B valuation entry. Wait for the S-1 to confirm revenue run rate and gross margin trajectory before sizing up. Don't go full position at open.
responses.reasoning: As a bull analyst, I need to establish the investment thesis clearly: strategic partnerships as demand floor, developer ecosystem stickiness, and the regulatory moat argument. I'm bullish but disciplined — recommending staged entry rather than all-in at IPO open, which gives me credibility with skeptics on the panel.

responses.action: Crosses arms, leans back in chair, slides a printout of dot-com era valuations across the table
responses.speech: Fifty to eighty billion dollars. For a company that burns cash faster than a GPU burns watts. Let me tell you what I see here — I see Pets.com with a PhD. Anthropic's entire business model rests on the assumption that compute costs will fall fast enough, and enterprise willingness to pay will rise fast enough, to close a gap that is currently a canyon. Meanwhile you've got Google — who is also a strategic investor, mind you, which is its own governance red flag — deploying Gemini across every product they own at zero marginal cost to the end user. OpenAI has Microsoft's entire distribution network. Meta is open-sourcing models that Anthropic charges API fees for. And Dario is out here talking about AI safety like it's a feature, not a liability on the P&L. I've been doing this thirty years. I watched Webvan raise a billion dollars and I watched it go to zero. I will not be buying Anthropic at IPO. I'll wait eighteen months, watch the lock-up expiration trigger insider selling, and revisit at a fifty percent haircut — if the company still exists in its current form.
responses.reasoning: Classic late-stage bubble dynamics: massive private valuation seeking public exit before profitability is demonstrated, strategic investors with conflicting incentives, and a moat that competitors are actively bulldozing. The safety-first positioning may be admirable but it structurally slows product velocity vs. OpenAI. Retail investors will be the exit liquidity for early VCs.

responses.action: Opens a spreadsheet and starts comparing Anthropic's projected valuation multiples against current AI company comps, while pulling up his Rivian and Palantir trade history as a reminder
responses.speech: Okay so here's my thing — I use Claude Code literally every single day. It's genuinely the best dev tool I've touched in years. But I also said that about Rivian's trucks and Palantir's data platform, and I bought both at IPO and watched them crater 60% before recovering. The product being great and the stock being a good buy on day one are completely different questions. $50-80B valuation range is a massive spread — that uncertainty alone tells me the banks don't really know what this is worth. Not profitable, Google and Amazon both have board influence AND competing products, and Dario is optimizing for safety which I actually respect but Wall Street is going to price that as 'slower growth.' I'm setting a calendar reminder for 6 months post-IPO when the lock-up expires. That's when I actually want to look at this.
responses.reasoning: My Rivian and Palantir losses taught me that early adopter enthusiasm doesn't translate to IPO pricing discipline. The lock-up expiration is a known catalyst for selling pressure — early employees and investors cashing out. That's historically a better entry point for retail. I love the product but I need to separate that from the investment thesis.
